# How to Do Great Work
**By Paul Graham (July 2021)**
*Source: http://paulgraham.com/greatwork.html*

[This is a condensed version of Paul Graham's comprehensive essay on doing great work. Full essay available at the link above.]

---

If you want to do great work, it helps to understand what great work is. The best way to understand it is to look at examples of great work.

Great work has two parts: doing something, and doing it well. You can't do great work without doing something, and you can't do it well without understanding what "well" means for that thing.

## Finding Great Work

When you're trying to find what to work on, you're really trying to find your personal optimum of three overlapping circles:

1. **What you're good at**
2. **What you enjoy**
3. **What makes an impact**

The ideal work for you is at the intersection of all three. If you're lucky, you'll find work that's naturally at the intersection. If not, you may have to push one of the circles toward overlap.

### What You're Good At

You have natural abilities. These are things you do better than most people without trying. They're your comparative advantage.

But here's the trap: most people's natural abilities are also their natural interests. Usually, the things you're good at are the things you enjoy. This makes the problem easier, but also makes it harder to recognize — because when you're good at something, it feels easy, and you discount it.

**The mistake people make**: they look at what they're good at and think "I can't make a career out of this, it's too easy." But ease is exactly the signal you're looking for.

### What You Enjoy

Work you don't enjoy is rarely great. The energy required to do great work is enormous, and you can't sustain that energy if you don't enjoy it.

But people are often confused about what they enjoy. They think they enjoy the praise they get for doing something, when what they actually enjoy is the work itself. Or they think they enjoy something because they're good at it, when they actually just enjoy being good at things.

Here's a test: if you couldn't tell anyone what you were doing, would you still want to do it?

### What Makes an Impact

Great work is work that makes a difference. It changes something. It creates something new. It opens up possibilities.

The highest-impact work tends to have these characteristics:
- **It's fundamental** — It affects the layer underneath, not just the layer on top
- **It's broadly applicable** — It affects many people or problems
- **It's durable** — It still matters 10 years from now

## The Shape of Great Work

Great work often looks different from ordinary work. It's not just better — it's qualitatively different.

### Independent

Great work tends to be more independent of existing work. It's not just an incremental improvement on what came before. It opens up a new direction.

The people who do great work often seem to be doing something obviously wrong — until it turns out they were right.

### Original

Great work is more original than ordinary work. But this is a tricky quality because originality is mostly detected in retrospect. When something is truly original, it often looks wrong or stupid or both.

The test: if your idea doesn't seem obviously wrong to most people, maybe it's not original enough.

### Hard

Great work is usually harder than ordinary work. But it's not just harder in degree — it's harder in kind. It's the kind of hard where you have to figure out what to do, not just do it.

The people who do great work tend to seek out problems that are harder than they know how to solve. They find this energizing rather than daunting.

## Learning What's Possible

One of the most important things you can do is learn what's possible. Most people have a very limited idea of what's possible, and this limits them more than any lack of ability.

When you see someone do something, you realize it's possible. Until then, you literally couldn't imagine it. This is why being around people who do great work is so valuable.

If you're trying to do great work, you should:
- Read the best work in your field
- Look at what the best people are doing
- Try to understand what makes it different from ordinary work

## The Process

Great work doesn't happen in a flash of insight. It comes from a process:

1. **Notice something** — A pattern, a gap, a question, an anomaly
2. **Get curious** — Why is this interesting? What does it mean?
3. **Dig deeper** — Follow the thread wherever it leads
4. **Try things** — Build prototypes, test hypotheses, make experiments
5. **Iterate** — Refine what works, abandon what doesn't
6. **Ship** — Get it out into the world

The people who do great work tend to be people who follow this process repeatedly. They're constantly noticing things, getting curious, and following threads.

### The Importance of Projects

Great work usually comes from working on a project. A project is a container for your curiosity. It gives you a reason to learn things, try things, and iterate.

Without a project, curiosity is just daydreaming. With a project, curiosity becomes work.

Good projects have these properties:
- They're specific enough to be tractable
- They're general enough to be interesting
- They're ambitious enough to be worth doing
- They're achievable enough to be worth starting

### The Importance of Peers

Great work tends to cluster. The people who do great work tend to know each other, work with each other, and inspire each other.

If you want to do great work, get to know the people doing great work in your field. Not just their work, but them. Go to conferences. Send emails. Ask questions. Be interested.

The best peers will:
- Challenge your ideas
- Teach you things you didn't know
- Connect you to other people
- Model what great work looks like

## What Great Work Looks Like

How do you know if you're doing great work? Here are some signs:

### You're Learning

Great work involves continuous learning. If you're not learning, you're probably not doing great work.

### It's Hard But Not Frustrating

Great work is hard, but it's not frustrating. When you're doing great work, the difficulty feels like an interesting problem, not an obstacle.

### Time Disappears

When you're doing great work, you lose track of time. You're not watching the clock. You're absorbed in the work.

### People Are Surprised

Great work surprises people. Not necessarily in a flashy way, but in a "I didn't expect that to work" way.

### It Opens New Possibilities

Great work doesn't just solve a problem — it opens up new problems or new approaches. It creates more work, not less.

---

## Key Takeaways for Your AI Agent Startup

1. **Find the intersection** — You need to be good at it, enjoy it, AND it needs to matter. All three circles.

2. **Ease is a signal** — The things you're naturally good at feel easy. That's not a bug, it's a feature.

3. **Learn what's possible** — Your biggest limitation is often not ability, but imagination. Be around people doing great work.

4. **Projects give curiosity direction** — Don't just be curious. Be curious about something specific that you're trying to build.

5. **Seek hardness of the right kind** — Great work is hard because you're figuring out what to do, not because you're grinding through known problems.

6. **Time disappears when it's right** — If you're watching the clock, you might be in the wrong work.

---

*This is a condensed summary. The full essay is much more comprehensive and worth reading in its entirety at paulgraham.com/greatwork.html*

# Superlinear Returns
**By Paul Graham (May 2024)**
*Source: http://paulgraham.com/superlinear.html*

---

Most people are accustomed to linear returns. If you work twice as hard, you get twice as much done. If you work three times as hard, you get three times as much done.

But some of the most important things in life have superlinear returns. If you work twice as hard, you don't just get twice as much done — you might get 10x or 100x as much done, or you might find that you're working on an entirely different level.

## What Superlinear Returns Look Like

Imagine a graph with effort on the x-axis and results on the y-axis. With linear returns, this is a straight line. With superlinear returns, the line curves upward — the more effort you put in, the steeper the line gets.

Superlinear returns show up in many domains:

### In Business

The classic example is startups. If you start a company, the returns are not linear. If you do slightly better than average, you might not get slightly better returns — you might get zero returns, because the company fails. But if you do really well, you might get returns that are orders of magnitude better than average.

This is why startups are weird. The difference between doing really well and doing slightly well is not slightly better outcomes — it's between a successful company and a failed company. And the difference between doing really well and doing phenomenally well is again another order of magnitude.

### In Learning

If you learn twice as much, you don't just know twice as much. You know things that you couldn't have known otherwise. Knowledge compounds.

This is why some people seem to learn much faster than others. It's not that they're learning at a linearly faster rate. It's that they've reached a point where knowledge compounds, and they're on a steeper part of the curve.

### In Networks

The value of a network is proportional to the square of the number of users — Metcalfe's law. This is a classic example of superlinear returns.

But the same principle applies to social networks of people. If you know twice as many people, you don't get twice as many opportunities. You get many more, because you can connect people to each other.

## The Implications

Superlinear returns have some counterintuitive implications.

### Outliers Matter More Than Averages

In a world with linear returns, the average tells you a lot. If returns are linear and some people are doing well and some are doing poorly, the average is a good summary.

But in a world with superlinear returns, the average tells you almost nothing. The outlier at the top might have results that are orders of magnitude better than everyone else's combined.

This is why successful people often seem like outliers — because they are. In domains with superlinear returns, success doesn't look like being slightly above average. It looks like being in a completely different category.

### The Best Strategy Might Be Extreme

In a linear world, the best strategy is often moderation. You want to be somewhere in the middle — not too risky, not too safe.

But in a world with superlinear returns, the best strategy might be extreme. If you're going to work hard, really work hard. If you're going to start a company, really commit. Half measures might get you nothing.

### Small Differences Compound

In a linear world, small differences don't matter much. If you're 10% better, you get 10% more results.

But in a world with superlinear returns, small differences can compound into huge differences. That 10% advantage might compound into being 10x better over time.

This is why people who seem slightly ahead can pull away over time. They're not just slightly ahead — they're on a steeper part of the curve, and the distance is growing.

## Where Superlinear Returns Exist

Superlinear returns appear in domains where:

1. **There are economies of scale** — The more you have, the easier it is to get more
2. **There are winner-take-all effects** — The best gets much more than the second best
3. **There are network effects** — The value grows with the square of users
4. **There is compounding** — Knowledge, relationships, and reputation compound
5. **There are threshold effects** — You need to cross a threshold before results appear

Some domains with superlinear returns:
- Startups and investments
- Learning and expertise
- Reputation and audience building
- Networks and relationships
- Technology development

Some domains with more linear returns:
- Hourly wage work
- Most traditional jobs
- Activities without economies of scale

## How to Take Advantage

If you want to take advantage of superlinear returns:

### Find Domains With Superlinear Returns

The first step is to work in a domain that has superlinear returns. Startups have superlinear returns. Learning has superlinear returns. Building an audience has superlinear returns.

Hourly wage work does not. If you're in a domain with linear returns, consider whether you can move to a domain with superlinear returns.

### Focus on the Outlier Cases

In a domain with superlinear returns, the average case is not that interesting. The interesting cases are the outliers — the people and companies that are way up the curve.

Study these outliers. What did they do differently? What allowed them to reach the steep part of the curve?

### Be Willing to Go All In

In a domain with superlinear returns, half measures might get you nothing. There might be a threshold you need to cross before any results appear.

This is why successful founders often seem obsessed. They're not just working hard — they're trying to cross the threshold. And they know that if they don't cross it, they get nothing.

### Think in Exponents, Not Increments

In a linear world, you think in increments: "How can I be 10% better?"

In a world with superlinear returns, you think in exponents: "How can I be 10x better?" or "How can I reach a steeper part of the curve?"

The right question is not "How can I do slightly more?" The right question is "How can I get on a different curve?"

### Understand That Results Are Unfair

In domains with superlinear returns, results seem unfair. Two people can work similarly hard and get wildly different results, because one is on a steeper part of the curve.

This is why people who don't understand superlinear returns think success is mostly luck. They see people working similarly hard with wildly different results, and conclude it must be luck. But it might just be that one of them crossed a threshold while the other didn't.

---

## Key Takeaways for Your AI Agent Startup

1. **The ELK debugging space might have superlinear returns** — If you build something that truly transforms how engineers work, the returns aren't linear. The best tool could dominate.

2. **Outliers matter more than averages** — Don't optimize for being slightly better. Optimize for being fundamentally different.

3. **Small differences compound** — If you're 10% better at understanding ELK pipelines, that might compound over time into being 10x better overall.

4. **Be willing to go all in** — In domains with superlinear returns, half measures might get you nothing. The threshold matters.

5. **Think in exponents** — Don't ask "How can I do 10% more?" Ask "How can I get on a steeper curve?"

6. **Results seem unfair** — Two people working similarly hard can get wildly different results. Understanding superlinear returns helps you see why.

---

*Read the full essay at paulgraham.com/superlinear.html*

# Naval on Wealth Creation
**Key principles from The Almanack of Naval Ravikant**
*Source: nav.al / The Almanack of Naval Ravikant by Eric Jorgenson*

---

## The Foundations

Naval Ravikant is an entrepreneur, investor, and philosopher. His thoughts on wealth creation have become essential reading for founders. Here are the key principles.

### Making Wealth Is a Skill, Not Luck

> "Making money is not a thing you do; it's a skill you learn."

Many people believe wealth is a matter of luck — being in the right place at the right time. Naval argues that wealth creation is a learnable skill that compounds over time. The people who "got lucky" often made their own luck through specific knowledge, good judgment, and understanding of leverage.

### Productize Yourself

> "Productize yourself. You are not going to get rich renting out your time. You must own equity — a piece of a business — to gain your financial freedom."

You cannot get wealthy by selling your time at an hourly rate. Time is finite and linear. To build wealth, you need:
- **Equity** — Ownership in a business
- **Product** — Something that scales without your direct time
- **Yourself** — Your unique knowledge, skills, and position

Combine all three: **Productize what's uniquely you.**

---

## Specific Knowledge

### What It Is

> "Specific knowledge is found by pursuing your genuine curiosity rather than what's 'hot' right now."

Specific knowledge is knowledge that cannot be easily taught or trained. It's knowledge that:
- You learned through apprenticeship or self-teaching
- Cannot be written down in a book or course
- Often looks like a quirk or obsession
- Is usually discovered by pursuing genuine curiosity

**Examples:**
- A salesperson's intuition about when to close
- A programmer's feel for clean architecture
- A founder's sense for product-market fit
- An engineer's deep understanding of a specific system

### Why It Matters

Specific knowledge is your competitive advantage. It can't be outsourced or automated because it's specific to you. It can't be taught in schools because it's too specific and context-dependent.

**For your ELK agent startup:** Your specific knowledge is that you've lived the pain of maintaining ELK stacks. You understand the hair-on-fire problems that generic engineers don't see. That's your specific knowledge.

### How to Build It

1. **Follow genuine curiosity** — Not what's "hot," but what genuinely interests you
2. **Apprentice with masters** — Learn from the best in your field
3. **Build in public** — Create a body of work that demonstrates your knowledge
4. **Embrace the weird** — Specific knowledge often looks like an obsession to others

---

## Leverage

### Three Types of Leverage

Leverage is what multiplies your output. There are three types:

| Type | Permission Needed? | Source | Scale |
|------|---------------------|--------|-------|
| **Labor** | Yes (need to hire) | People working for you | Linear |
| **Capital** | Yes (need money) | Money working for you | Superlinear |
| **Code** | No | Software working for you | Exponential |
| **Media** | No | Content working for you | Exponential |

### Labor and Capital vs. Code and Media

**Labor and Capital require permission.** You need people to agree to work for you. You need investors to give you money.

**Code and Media do not require permission.** You can write software on your laptop. You can create content and publish it. No one can stop you.

This is why code and media are the highest leverage activities. They're permissionless leverage.

**Code:** Write a piece of software once, it can serve millions of users. Marginal cost of replication is near zero.

**Media:** Create content once, it can reach millions of people. Podcasts, blogs, videos — all scale without marginal cost.

### The Best Founders Use All Four

The best founders combine all four types of leverage:
- **Labor:** A small, excellent team
- **Capital:** Funding or revenue to invest
- **Code:** Software that scales
- **Media:** Content that spreads their message

---

## Accountability

### Why It Matters

> "Take accountability for your work. If you fail, you want the failure attributed to you. Not because you like failing, but because you want to be able to fix it."

Accountability gives you:
- **Learning** — When things fail and it's attributed to you, you can learn and fix
- **Trust** — People trust those who take accountability
- **Asymmetric risk** — If you succeed, you get outsized credit; if you fail, you can try again

### The Accountability Leak

If you're succeeding but not getting credit, you have an accountability leak. Someone is taking credit for your work. Fix the leak.

If you're failing but not being blamed, you have an accountability insulation. You're not learning from failure. Remove the insulation.

---

## Judgment

### The CEO Skill

Judgment is the ability to make good decisions with incomplete information. This is the primary skill of CEOs and investors.

Judgment comes from:
- **Experience** — Making many decisions and seeing the outcomes
- **Pattern matching** — Recognizing situations from past experience
- **Mental models** — Frameworks for thinking about problems

### Judgment vs. Knowledge

Knowledge is knowing facts. Judgment is knowing what to do with those facts.

You can have all the knowledge in the world and still have poor judgment. Judgment is developed through making decisions, seeing outcomes, and learning from mistakes.

**For your ELK agent startup:** You have knowledge of ELK. But judgment is knowing which problems are worth solving, which users are worth targeting, and which features are worth building.

---

## The Wealth Creation Formula

### To Create Wealth

> "Wealth is assets that earn while you sleep."

Combine:
1. **Specific knowledge** — What only you know
2. **Leverage** — Code, media, capital, labor
3. **Accountability** — Taking ownership of outcomes
4. **Judgment** — Making good decisions

### The Pattern

1. **Learn** — Develop specific knowledge in a domain
2. **Build** — Create a product that leverages that knowledge
3. **Own** — Take equity, not just salary
4. **Scale** — Use code and media for permissionless leverage

---

## Wealth vs. Money vs. Status

### Definitions

- **Wealth** is assets that earn while you sleep. Businesses, code, media, investments.
- **Money** is how we transfer wealth. Money is the medium of exchange.
- **Status** is your position in the social hierarchy. Status is a zero-sum game.

### The Trap

Many people pursue status instead of wealth. Status is zero-sum — for you to move up, someone else has to move down. This creates conflict.

Wealth is positive-sum — you can create wealth without taking from others. When you build a useful product, you've created wealth. Both you and your customers are better off.

**For your ELK agent startup:** You're creating wealth by building something that helps engineers. You're not taking from anyone. This is positive-sum.

---

## Key Quotes

> "Seek wealth, not money or status. Wealth is having assets that earn while you sleep. Money is how we transfer time and wealth. Status is your place in the social hierarchy."

> "You will get rich by giving society what it wants but does not yet know how to get. At scale."

> "The internet has massively broadened the possible space of careers. Most people haven't figured this out yet."

> "Specific knowledge is often highly technical or creative. It cannot be outsourced or automated."

> "_CODE_ and _MEDIA_ are the permissionless lever. You can write software, and it can run while you sleep. You can create content and it can serve millions."

---

## Key Takeaways for Your AI Agent Startup

1. **Your specific knowledge is your lived pain** — You've maintained ELK stacks. You know the problems. That's your specific knowledge.

2. **Use code and media for leverage** — Build an AI agent (code) and share what you learn (media). Both are permissionless.

3. **Take accountability** — Own the problems you solve. If your agent fails, fix it. If it succeeds, get credit.

4. **Build judgment through decisions** — Every choice about what to build is a judgment call. Make many, learn from the outcomes.

5. **Wealth is assets that earn while you sleep** — Your goal is not hourly consulting work. It's an agent that can solve problems without your direct involvement.

6. **Give society what it wants but doesn't yet know how to get** — ELK engineers want debugging help. They don't know how to get an AI agent that does it. You're building that.

---

*For more, read The Almanack of Naval Ravikant (free PDF online) or listen to his podcast appearances.*

I met Dinesh Raju about 10 years ago, at a startup event that I no longer remember. In that first meeting he gave me an early version of what I now call ‘[beware what sounds insightful](https://commoncog.com/beware-what-sounds-insightful/)’, but he was talking about startup advice, not online writing. I listened very carefully (and this is quite obvious, given that I wrote [this post](https://commoncog.com/dont-take-generic-business-advice-from-vcs/) on VCs, years later) and internalised it in the decade that followed.
Much later, when I was already running operations in Vietnam, I came to him for management advice. He told me to reread Ray Dalio’s [Principles](https://commoncog.com/the-principles-sequence/), and to work my way through [High Output Management](https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/324750.High_Output_Management?ref=commoncog.com). I did both. About a year after that, I came back and told him I was much better as a manager, that retention was high and morale was great, and he gave me a link to his [company](https://www.referralcandy.com/?ref=commoncog.com)’s management doc. He told me to pay special attention to the section titled ‘prioritise the highest order bit’.
I thought it was pretty good.
I met up with Dinesh again a couple of weeks ago. He said something along the lines of “I’ve always found the highest order bit idea to be profound, and the older I get, the more I realise how profound and true it is” — so I went and looked up his management doc again.
The phrase ‘highest order bit’ isn’t new by any means (Bill Gates [used it](https://hardcoresoftware.learningbyshipping.com/p/018-microsofts-two-bountiful-gardens?ref=commoncog.com) in Microsoft in the early 90s, so presumably it was in the air before that), but I thought that Dinesh’s articulation was particularly useful. I quote, from his doc:

Prioritise the Highest Order Bit

When evaluating past decisions or thinking about making new ones, a useful analogy to use is the flipping of the highest-order bit.

The binary representation of a number is made up of a set of ordered bits. The number 13 would be represented as 1101.

The increase in value of flipping a higher-order bit outweighs flipping all the lower-order bits combined. 100 is bigger than 011, 1000 is bigger than 0111, and so on.

So aim to identify and flip the highest order bit because if you don't, you're not going to be able to make up for it by flipping everything else.

An entrepreneur may look at a successful diner and think that customers are there because of the hip decor on the walls. She sets up a competing diner across the street with better decor only to find that it can't pull any customers away. The highest-order bit isn't the decor. It's actually the cheap but high-quality coffee that customers care most about. Without getting the coffee right, no amount of aesthetics will beat the competition.

In the real world, it gets more complicated since bits are intimately linked to one another. Flipping a higher-order bit may reveal a string of unflipped lower-order bits, or may even require unflipping lower bits first because of resource constraints. Customers at your diner really want home delivery. But you'll need to buy a van for that, and to afford it, you're going to have to sell some of the decor.

Many of the flaws you see in a startup are lower-order bits which were left unaddressed or even deliberately unflipped, so that higher-order ones could be flipped first.

So far, this is pretty standard stuff. The highest order bit implies that you want to always be working on the most important thing. Almost by definition, the most important thing is the thing that moves the needle for your work, not necessarily the thing that is most tractable right now.
But the document has a second section, that adds additional nuance to the idea:
Get to the ideal state by iterating relentlesslyGiven the importance of the highest-order bit, it might be tempting to drop everything else and focus only on flipping it first. If our breakfast diner's signature coffee is bland, shouldn't we stop trying to improve the decor or serve more customers until the coffee is fixed? Unfortunately it's not that simple.
- You don't always know which bit is the highest-order one, how to flip it, or even what the flipped version looks like. This should be unsurprising, because pretty much no one else knows either. In startups especially, what we 'know' is the [product of generalisations](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=4967964&ref=commoncog.com) and communications about half-truths from the environment we're in. It takes time to develop the intuition to see bits in the right order and how to flip them. You develop this intuition by producing more work and flipping more bits, even if they end up being lower-order ones. Speed up the process by constantly getting in sync with people whose opinions are more accurate than yours.
- You don't always have the right resources to flip the highest-order bit. Even the most successful companies in the world are limited by resource constraints. To get more resources, you'll need to flip some lower-order bits first. Even though these improvements will be of the lower-order variety, they show growth and potential. This growth begets more resources which can then be used to target higher-order bits.
Hang on to the irritation you feel when you see all the ways that the bits in your environment aren't lined up. You feel this because [you have taste](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E1oZhEIrer4&ref=commoncog.com).  Safeguard your taste, but don't let it grind you to a halt. Instead, channel that emotion. Work on all the bits you have the ability to flip with the resources and skills you bring to the table. Fix them the best you can while still seeding and driving new initiatives, even lower-order ones. We ultimately want to build exemplary awe-inspiring products, and we will. But understand that the only practical way to get there is by iterating relentlessly through interim states that are uninspiring but improving.
All this means that you need to be comfortable with making decisions which leaves many bits unflipped or even cause flipped bits to regress. You'll need to do this even while being burdened with flaws from decisions made by others in the past.
Startup decision-making is therefore an exercise in:
1. Inheriting an environment that has many shocking flaws.
2. Identifying the highest-order flaw that you have the ability to address with your limited resources.
3. Picking the best available action to fix it, even if it risks making some lower-order flaws worse.
4. Starting again from step 1 with, hopefully, a less egregious set of shortcomings to choose from.
And I realise that this bit is what best explains the chaos of building an early-stage startup.

## Playing House
Here’s an example. I remember one time, late in 2017, when we had revenue problems in my old company. (An explanation of how this came to be would take too long; suffice to say it had something to do with an abrupt change in the market that occurred in second half of that year). I was due to leave the business, so my boss and I sat down for dinner, and I pointed out a half dozen time bombs with the way we were managing our team. I said he needed to fix that or risk having people leave the company. But he disagreed.
“Look I understand, all of that is bad, but what’s worse is our company running out of money. I need to solve this first.”
He turned out to be right.
But of course I was also right  — I was good enough at management at that point to predict the problems correctly. In the end, we took a huge hit on morale, and a bunch of good people left. It took the company a year before they recovered. My boss was ok with this. The highest order bit was ensuring that the company survived, never mind that certain ‘bits regressed’, as Dinesh would put it. Rebuilding that revenue took all of his attention for the next year; he willingly let other problems go.
I’ve often said that the biggest thing I learnt from my old boss was cash discipline — or at least, an understanding of what cash discipline looks like. We were bootstrapped, and we sold actual hardware — which meant two big things: first, we could only spend what we sold, and second, a chunk of our cash was always tied up in inventory. My boss’s focus on cash flow meant that we would often take shortcuts on lots of other things. Technical debt? All the time. No proper user testing for months at a stretch? Yep. Ignore certain customers, and risk burning our relationships with them because a more important customer needed help? That too. We patched up the processes over time so they became less bad, but we were never 100% blowup free.
Much later, I learnt that company building at the expense of business building was a common failure mode for startups. Michael Seibel [says](https://www.joincolossus.com/episodes/34802825/seibel-lessons-from-thousands-of-startups?tab=transcript&ref=commoncog.com), of that tendency:
When you have product-market fit, you should do company building, but most people never get it. And most people should never be doing company building. And most people should never be investing their investor dollars in company building. They should just be investing in product. That's hard. Product can punch you in the face every day. And sometimes, you want something new, you want something different. If your products punch you in the face, but you're hiring great people, you can squint, lie to yourself, and tell yourself your company's doing well, until you run out of money.If you’ve never worked at a startup, and you wonder what it means when people say ‘every startup is a shit-show on the inside’, well — this is what it means. The advice to ‘focus on the highest order bit’ is easy to say, but the natural implication of putting it to practice, especially in a resource-constrained environment, is that you often have to let other things blow up. And Dinesh’s company management doc is the best articulation I’ve seen of this simple truth.

 Originally published 20 April 2021, last updated 29 April 2021.

 This article is part of the [Operations](/operations) topic cluster, which belongs to the [Business Expertise Triad](/business-expertise). Read more from this topic [here→](/operations)

## The thought of business school make you go ‘eww’?

 You’re in good company.

 9,000+ investors and operators read Commoncog to sharpen their business acumen ... WITHOUT going back to school.

# Prioritise the Highest Order Bit
**By Cédric Chin / Commoncog (April 2021)**
*Source: https://commoncog.com/highest-order-bit/*

---

I met Dinesh Raju about 10 years ago, at a startup event. In that first meeting he gave me an early version of what I now call 'beware what sounds insightful' — but he was talking about startup advice, not online writing.

Much later, when I was running operations in Vietnam, I came to him for management advice. He told me to reread Ray Dalio's Principles, and to work my way through High Output Management. About a year after that, I came back and told him I was much better as a manager, that retention was high and morale was great — and he gave me a link to his company's management doc. He told me to pay special attention to the section titled **'prioritise the highest order bit'.**

I thought it was pretty good.

The phrase 'highest order bit' isn't new (Bill Gates used it at Microsoft in the early 90s, so presumably it was in the air before that), but Dinesh's articulation was particularly useful.

---

## Prioritise the Highest Order Bit

When evaluating past decisions or thinking about making new ones, a useful analogy to use is the flipping of the highest-order bit.

The binary representation of a number is made up of a set of ordered bits. The number 13 would be represented as 1101.

**The increase in value of flipping a higher-order bit outweighs flipping all the lower-order bits combined. 100 is bigger than 011, 1000 is bigger than 0111, and so on.**

So aim to identify and flip the highest order bit because if you don't, you're not going to be able to make up for it by flipping everything else.

### The Diner Example

An entrepreneur may look at a successful diner and think that customers are there because of the hip decor on the walls. She sets up a competing diner across the street with better decor only to find that it can't pull any customers away.

The highest-order bit isn't the decor. **It's actually the cheap but high-quality coffee that customers care most about.** Without getting the coffee right, no amount of aesthetics will beat the competition.

In the real world, it gets more complicated since bits are intimately linked to one another. Flipping a higher-order bit may reveal a string of unflipped lower-order bits, or may even require unflipping lower bits first because of resource constraints.

Customers at your diner really want home delivery. But you'll need to buy a van for that, and to afford it, you're going to have to sell some of the decor.

**Many of the flaws you see in a startup are lower-order bits which were left unaddressed or even deliberately unflipped, so that higher-order ones could be flipped first.**

---

## Get to the Ideal State by Iterating Relentlessly

Given the importance of the highest-order bit, it might be tempting to drop everything else and focus only on flipping it first. If our breakfast diner's signature coffee is bland, shouldn't we stop trying to improve the decor or serve more customers until the coffee is fixed?

**Unfortunately it's not that simple.**

### Three Complications

**1. You don't always know which bit is the highest-order one.**

You don't know how to flip it, or even what the flipped version looks like. This should be unsurprising, because pretty much no one else knows either. In startups especially, what we 'know' is the product of generalisations and half-truths from the environment we're in.

It takes time to develop the intuition to see bits in the right order and how to flip them. **You develop this intuition by producing more work and flipping more bits, even if they end up being lower-order ones.** Speed up the process by constantly getting in sync with people whose opinions are more accurate than yours.

**2. You don't always have the right resources to flip the highest-order bit.**

Even the most successful companies in the world are limited by resource constraints. To get more resources, you'll need to flip some lower-order bits first.

Even though these improvements will be of the lower-order variety, they show growth and potential. This growth begets more resources which can then be used to target higher-order bits.

**3. Iterate through uninspiring interim states.**

Hang on to the irritation you feel when you see all the ways that the bits in your environment aren't lined up. You feel this because you have taste. Safeguard your taste, but don't let it grind you to a halt.

Instead, channel that emotion. Work on all the bits you have the ability to flip with the resources and skills you bring to the table. Fix them the best you can while still seeding and driving new initiatives, even lower-order ones.

**We ultimately want to build exemplary awe-inspiring products, and we will. But understand that the only practical way to get there is by iterating relentlessly through interim states that are uninspiring but improving.**

---

## The Startup Decision-Making Framework

All this means that you need to be comfortable with making decisions which leaves many bits unflipped or even cause flipped bits to regress. You'll need to do this even while being burdened with flaws from decisions made by others in the past.

**Startup decision-making is therefore an exercise in:**

1. Inheriting an environment that has many shocking flaws
2. Identifying the highest-order flaw that you have the ability to address with your limited resources
3. Picking the best available action to fix it, even if it risks making some lower-order flaws worse
4. Starting again from step 1 with, hopefully, a less egregious set of shortcomings to choose from

And I realise that this bit is what best explains the chaos of building an early-stage startup.

---

## Playing House: A Real Example

In late 2017, we had revenue problems in my old company. I was due to leave the business, so my boss and I sat down for dinner. I pointed out a half dozen time bombs with the way we were managing our team. I said he needed to fix that or risk having people leave the company.

But he disagreed.

"Look I understand, all of that is bad, but what's worse is our company running out of money. I need to solve this first."

**He turned out to be right.**

But of course I was also right — I was good enough at management to predict the problems correctly. In the end, we took a huge hit on morale, and a bunch of good people left. It took the company a year before they recovered.

My boss was ok with this. **The highest order bit was ensuring that the company survived, never mind that certain 'bits regressed'.** Rebuilding that revenue took all of his attention for the next year; he willingly let other problems go.

### Cash Discipline

I've often said that the biggest thing I learnt from my old boss was cash discipline — or at least, an understanding of what cash discipline looks like. We were bootstrapped, and we sold actual hardware, which meant:
- First, we could only spend what we sold
- Second, a chunk of our cash was always tied up in inventory

My boss's focus on cash flow meant that we would often take shortcuts on lots of other things:
- Technical debt? All the time
- No proper user testing for months at a stretch? Yep
- Ignore certain customers because a more important customer needed help? That too

We patched up the processes over time so they became less bad, but we were never 100% blowup free.

### Company Building vs Business Building

Much later, I learnt that company building at the expense of business building was a common failure mode for startups. Michael Seibel (Y Combinator) said:

> "When you have product-market fit, you should do company building, but most people never get it. And most people should never be doing company building. And most people should never be investing their investor dollars in company building. They should just be investing in product. That's hard. Product can punch you in the face every day."

If you've never worked at a startup, and you wonder what it means when people say 'every startup is a shit-show on the inside' — well, this is what it means.

**The advice to 'focus on the highest order bit' is easy to say, but the natural implication of putting it to practice, especially in a resource-constrained environment, is that you often have to let other things blow up.**

---

## Key Takeaways for Your AI Agent Startup

1. **Identify your highest-order bit** — For ELK engineers, is it pipeline debugging? Is it on-call response? Is it building new pipelines? What matters most?

2. **You can't compensate with lower-order bits** — You can't make up for getting the coffee wrong with better decor. If your agent doesn't solve the real pain, nothing else matters.

3. **You don't always know the highest bit** — You develop intuition by working, by talking to users, by iterating. Get in sync with people whose opinions are more accurate than yours.

4. **Resources constrain which bits you can flip** — Sometimes you need to flip lower-order bits to get resources for higher-order ones. That's okay.

5. **Accept chaos and regression** — Startups are shit-shows because they're always letting lower-order bits blow up while focusing on the highest-order bit. This is normal.

6. **Business building > company building** — Don't build company (hiring, process, culture) at the expense of business (product-market fit, revenue, customers). Most startups should be doing product work, not company work.

---

John D. Rockefeller Sr. was the richest tycoon of the Gilded Age, at one point valued at two percent of the United States’s GDP. His rise coincided with the creation of the ‘trust’ structure of business — a structure that enabled business practices so unfair and monopolistic the US Government had to create the Sherman antitrust act of 1890 to reign it in.
But the rise of Rockefeller and his ilk also prompted the rise of the muckrakers — reform-minded journalists that exposed business leaders and politicians as corrupt. In fact, the muckrakers were influential in turning the tide of public opinion against the trusts, which led, indirectly, to the creation of the Sherman antitrust act. This was the dawn of the age of the newspaper, and the rise of journalism as a discipline alongside it.
But like most young mediums, journalism, too, had its excesses.
[Ida Tarbell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ida_Tarbell?ref=commoncog.com) was Rockefeller’s muckraker nemesis, and a leading activist of the Progressive Era. She is often credited with pioneering the practice of investigative journalism. But Tarbell stretched the truth about Rockefeller numerous times over the course of her reporting. As Ron Chernow writes in [Titan](https://www.amazon.com/Titan-Life-John-Rockefeller-Sr/dp/1400077303?ref=commoncog.com), his history of the Rockefeller family and their business dealings:
Although Tarbell pretended to apply her scalpel to Standard Oil with surgical objectivity, she was never neutral and not only because of her father. Her brother, William Walter Tarbell, had been a leading figure in forming the Pure Oil Company, the most serious domestic challenger to Standard Oil, and his letters to her were laced with anti-Standard venom. Complaining of the trust’s price manipulations in one letter, Will warned her, “Some of those fellows will get killed one of those days.” As Pure Oil’s treasurer in 1902, Will steered legions of Rockefeller enemies to his sister and even vetted her manuscripts. Far from cherishing her neutrality, Tarbell in the end adhered to the advice she had once received from Henry James: “Cherish your contempts.” Amazingly enough, nobody made an issue of Tarbell’s veritable partnership with her brother in exposing his chief competitor.And later in the book:
However pathbreaking in its time and richly deserving of its accolades, the Tarbell series does not, finally, stand up as an enduring piece of history. The more closely one examines it, the more it seems a superior screed masquerading as sober history. In the end, Tarbell could not conquer her nostalgia for the Titusville of her girlhood, that lost paradise of heroic friends and neighbors who went forth doughtily to do battle with the all-devouring Standard Oil dragon.I’ve left a lot out of the excerpts above, but anyone reading the history of Ida Tarbell and Standard Oil would be struck by how many of the tricks Tarbell used to stoke anger against Rockefeller still work today.
More importantly, however, those tricks aren't that surprising to us in 2019, more than 100 years after the rise of journalism. Even a casual reader of the news knows to be sceptical when reading a story in the papers, or watching a reported segment on cable news. But at the time of Tarbell’s campaign, public relations hadn’t yet been invented. Society had not developed antibodies against manipulative journalism.

## Being Critical in the Age of the Internet
If you read something on the internet today, the odds are good that the writing was produced to capture your attention. This isn’t some profound realisation, of course: many of us are familiar with the idea of an ‘attention economy’. We’re at the end of the second decade of the social Internet; the returns to attention are notoriously lucrative and the competition is steep.
I think the arms race for our attention has led to an arms race in writing. The best online writers are able to make something sound insightful — regardless of whether it’s true, or whether it’s useful.
On one hand, this is the writer’s job. [Jason Zweig](https://jasonzweig.com/?ref=commoncog.com) of the Wall Street Journal likes saying that his goal as a finance writer is to present a small handful of universal investing truths in increasingly novel ways, so readers never know that he’s repeating himself. But at another level, this shift means that online readers have to be increasingly critical when reading essays and blog posts for instrumental reasons.
This isn’t some evil conspiracy. ‘Writers optimising to produce insight porn to grab attention’ sounds nefarious, but it’s really more like ‘writers responding to the incentives of the social internet’ — a simple side effect of the attention economy.
And we shouldn’t be surprised: if Instagram has changed the language of photography, then we should also expect the emergence of Twitter, Medium and Google to change the tenor of writing.

## Tricks
It’s interesting to note that I’ve chosen ‘insight’ as a key metric to focus on. I’m ignoring tribalism and novelty here — though both are equally powerful ways to capture attention on the social internet. ‘Hot takes’ that appeal to a group identity perform better than opinion pieces that do not; news that is extreme, or new, or novel does better than news about what is natural and commonplace.
When you’re reading for career reasons, however, you’re seeking insights that can help you perform better or navigate the world better or see things differently or frame things more effectively. Naturally, writing in the self-help or career-advice genres will have to simulate insightfulness in order to keep your attention.
Some ideas sound insightful because they are true. But not all true ideas sound insightful. A true idea that is commonly accepted can sound trite and obvious: we call those clichés. The job of a good writer, then, is to present some truth in a way that doesn’t trip our cliché triggers.
This means that attention seeking in writing usually devolves to the same handful of tricks. I’ve used many of those tricks myself, here at Commonplace. This is a non-exhaustive list.
Use a story. I started this piece with a story. Preferably from a historical period that the reader isn’t familiar with. This establishes that the writer knows something that the reader doesn’t, and is communicating some argument or idea with a basis in history.
I’m not being completely facetious here. Arguments from historical example are often doubly effective: first because they are rooted in reality, and second because they allow the writer to tell a story. Readers love stories — they’re [far easier to read than argumentative prose](https://commoncog.com/the-land-and-expand-strategy-for-reading/).
Sometimes, writers present obvious truths by way of a story. Morgan Housel is a master of this: in [Fat, Happy, And In Over Your Head](https://www.collaborativefund.com/blog/fat-happy-and-in-over-your-head/?ref=commoncog.com) he tells two stories: the first of the co-working company WeWork and the second of the greatest ski racer in history, Marcel Hirscher. The point of the piece? Housel asserts that you can avoid the pain of loss aversion if you can somehow stop yourself from being greedy. The idea is simple, but Housel’s presentation is novel. Whether his assertion is practicable is another thing entirely.
Repackage obvious truths and sprinkle them over the course of an essay. If a cliché is a truth that is commonly accepted, then an insight is a truth that isn’t widespread. Clichés can thus be repackaged to sound insightful. This is a useful trick because a) clichés are often truths the reader already agrees with, and b) whatever sounds insightful will keep the reader going.
At the top of this essay, I wrote “if Instagram has changed the language of photography, then we should also expect the emergence of Twitter, Medium and Google to change the tenor of writing.”
This is a fairly obvious truth that’s been rewritten to sound more insightful. If you stop to think a bit, you’d quickly realise that writers have always optimised for attention. And if you read a lot online, you’ve probably already noticed this effect in action. It’s just the reference to Instagram that threw you. (Also: look at how I use the phrase ‘optimised for attention’ here, when I could have just as simply said ‘liked to be read’. Certain phrases sound more sophisticated than others — this is yet another technique. More on this in a bit.)
The odds are good, however, that when you read this sentence, you became curious. You kept scrolling because you wanted to see where I would go. This is the trick in action.
David Perell has a famous essay titled [Peter Thiel’s Religion](https://www.perell.com/blog/peter-thiel?ref=commoncog.com). The essay is the length of a small booklet, and is divided into tiny sections, each ending with a packaged insight. A reader going through the piece will keep scrolling, because their brain will go “Oh, that’s interesting! And that’s interesting! And that's also interesting! Ooh, what’s next?”
As a writer, I admire what he’s done. But as a business person, nearly everything that Perell says in the piece about business is subtly wrong — enough to make me treat his essay as entertainment, not education.
What sounds insightful isn't always true or useful.
Use more sophisticated language than necessary. Sometimes simple ideas can be dressed up to seem more legitimate than they really are. The problem is that there are times where this is a reasonable strategy. Economist Russ Roberts [once said](https://www.econtalk.org/andy-matuschak-on-books-and-learning/?ref=commoncog.com), of Nassim Nicholas Taleb:
Now, let me take a different book: Fooled by Randomness, by Nassim Taleb. There were two views of that book, which I've mentioned here before. One view was: “There is nothing original in this book. This guy is a fraud. He pretends he has figured out all this stuff.” And I said, “You know, I agree with that.” I didn't learn anything that I didn't know in this book before. I knew that probability is difficult. I knew that risk is a hard thing to wrap your mind around. I understand something about most of the ideas he talked about in the book. So, in some sense, I learned nothing. On the other hand, I learned something incredibly deep. That book really grabbed me by the guts and jerked me around. It forced me to confront some things that I ‘knew’ but didn't really internalise. And I would put that as another category of learning.Taleb’s strategy of using stories and sophisticated language (along with made-up names) is designed to hammer home a simple set of ideas. Those ideas are difficult to grasp at the explicit level. They have to be [made tacit](https://commoncog.com/the-mental-model-fallacy/) to be useful. So Taleb’s approach is a legitimate use of this trick.
Other times, however, if you strip away the complicated words, the underlying argument appears ridiculous.
Venkatesh Rao is one of the best Internet writers I know. But his pieces are often optimised to sound insightful, as opposed to capturing some operationally useful truth about the world.
In [CEOs don’t steer](https://www.ribbonfarm.com/2017/11/09/ceos-dont-steer/?ref=commoncog.com), Rao writes paragraphs like:
The primary CEO function, and the trait the good ones are selected for, is to provide the gyroscopic stability required to keep a company vectored in the chosen direction. They end up in the jobs they do because they counterbalance an organization’s natural tendency towards distraction, ADD and momentum dissipation. A typical company is a wandering, wobbling hive mind, liable to spend all its time chasing distractions if you let it, before dissolving into a bunch of clever tweets about crappy prototypes.This is a sophisticated sentence. It uses words like ‘gyroscopic stability’ and ‘vectored in the chosen direction’ and ‘clever tweets about crappy prototypes’ — a juxtaposition I enjoy.
But if you strip the essay of its sophisticated language, the core argument Rao makes is ridiculous: most CEOs are unintellectual dummies whose sole purpose is to keep the company on track, rowing in a single direction. This property, Rao asserts, is why so many business books are reducible to single-sentence epithets. It is why consultants cannot often successfully give advice to CEOs. It asserts that CEOs are selected for their ‘non-steering ability’, instead of some other factor. It even explains the existence of the CEO ‘reality distortion field’ (because, Rao asserts, non-steering is more effective in the presence of a vision). It is why companies change CEOs in times of crisis — which Rao argues in such a delightful paragraph that I can’t help but quote:
A “steering” event at the CEO level is a reorientation for the entire company, and for reasons I’ll get to, the best way to accomplish it is to replace the CEO with a new one who is already locked on to a more desirable orientation vector. Like say, Dara Khosrowshahi, who, based on his history, was apparently already locked on to the “don’t be a misogynist asshole” vector that Uber needed before he got the job.Stripped of all sophisticated language, this argument is easily dismissed as facetious. Each property that Rao pins on ‘not steering’ can be explained by a simpler reason. Rao is simply taking one facet of the CEO experience, and then extrapolating from it into an entire theory of the firm.
Rao’s piece is not ok if your goal is to read for career reasons. But it's ok if your goal is to read for entertainment. It’s ok because Rao’s goal is to attract eyeballs, not create better business leaders. And his writing is so good most people will forgive him for it.

## Beware What Sounds Insightful
The takeaway from this piece is that what sounds insightful and what is useful or true are often not the same thing. This aspect of critical reading isn’t emphasised as much as it should be — outside of, perhaps, the social sciences. I think we are all the poorer for it.
A year ago I wrote [Writing Doesn’t Make You a Genius](https://commoncog.com/writing-doesnt-make-you-a-genius/). I noticed that people tend to assume good writers are smarter than they actually are. I argued that this was mistaken — that writers sound smarter on paper because the act of writing forces them to clarify their ideas.
But now I have another reason. Writers are often seen as smarter because good writers today are trained to optimise for sounding insightful. This bleeds over into reader perception.
I think that whether a writer sounds smart or a piece sounds sophisticated shouldn’t affect you if your goal is to put things you read to practice. The questions remain the same: “Is this person [believable](https://commoncog.com/believability/)? [How likely](https://commoncog.com/the-hierarchy-of-practical-evidence/) is this going to be useful? And what’s the cheapest way to find out?”

 Originally published 24 September 2019, last updated 29 June 2021.

## The thought of business school make you go ‘eww’?

# Beware What Sounds Insightful
**By Cédric Chin / Commoncog (September 2019)**
*Source: https://commoncog.com/beware-what-sounds-insightful/*

---

John D. Rockefeller Sr. was the richest tycoon of the Gilded Age. The rise of Rockefeller and his ilk also prompted the rise of the muckrakers — reform-minded journalists that exposed business leaders and politicians as corrupt.

> "Although Tarbell pretended to apply her scalpel to Standard Oil with surgical objectivity, she was never neutral... Her brother, William Walter Tarbell, had been a leading figure in forming the Pure Oil Company, the most serious domestic challenger to Standard Oil."

The tricks Tarbell used to stoke anger against Rockefeller still work today. But at the time, society had not developed antibodies against manipulative journalism.

---

## Being Critical in the Age of the Internet

If you read something on the internet today, the odds are good that the writing was produced to capture your attention. This isn't some profound realisation: many of us are familiar with the idea of an 'attention economy'. We're at the end of the second decade of the social Internet.

But at another level, online readers have to be increasingly critical when reading essays and blog posts for instrumental reasons.

This isn't some evil conspiracy. 'Writers optimising to produce insight porn to grab attention' sounds nefarious, but it's really just 'writers responding to the incentives of the social internet' — a simple side effect of the attention economy.

---

## Tricks That Make Things Sound Insightful

### Trick 1: Use a Story

I started this piece with a story. Preferably from a historical period that the reader isn't familiar with. This establishes that the writer knows something that the reader doesn't, and is communicating some argument with a basis in history.

Arguments from historical example are often doubly effective: first because they are rooted in reality, and second because they allow the writer to tell a story. Readers love stories — they're far easier to read than argumentative prose.

Sometimes, writers present obvious truths by way of a story. Morgan Housel is a master of this. In *Fat, Happy, And In Over Your Head*, he tells two stories: the first of WeWork and the second of Marcel Hirscher. The point? You can avoid the pain of loss aversion if you stop yourself from being greedy.

The idea is simple, but Housel's presentation is novel. Whether his assertion is practicable is another thing entirely.

### Trick 2: Repackage Obvious Truths

If a cliché is a truth that is commonly accepted, then an insight is a truth that isn't widespread. Clichés can thus be repackaged to sound insightful.

This is useful because:
- Clichés are often truths the reader already agrees with
- Whatever sounds insightful will keep the reader going

David Perell has a famous essay titled *Peter Thiel's Religion*. The essay is the length of a small booklet, divided into tiny sections, each ending with a packaged insight. A reader going through will keep scrolling because their brain goes "Oh, that's interesting! And that's interesting! And that's also interesting!"

**As a writer, I admire what he's done. But as a business person, nearly everything that Perell says in the piece about business is subtly wrong — enough to make me treat his essay as entertainment, not education.**

### Trick 3: Use Sophisticated Language

Sometimes simple ideas can be dressed up to seem more legitimate than they really are.

Economist Russ Roberts once said of Nassim Nicholas Taleb:

> "One view [of Fooled by Randomness] was: 'There is nothing original in this book. I didn't learn anything that I didn't know.' On the other hand, I learned something incredibly deep. That book grabbed me by the guts and jerked me around. It forced me to confront some things that I 'knew' but didn't really internalise."

Taleb's strategy of using stories and sophisticated language is designed to hammer home a simple set of ideas. Those ideas are difficult to grasp at the explicit level. So Taleb's approach is a legitimate use of this trick.

**Other times, however, if you strip away the complicated words, the underlying argument appears ridiculous.**

Venkatesh Rao is one of the best Internet writers I know. But his pieces are often optimised to sound insightful, as opposed to capturing some operationally useful truth about the world.

In *CEOs don't steer*, Rao writes:

> "The primary CEO function, and the trait the good ones are selected for, is to provide the gyroscopic stability required to keep a company vectored in the chosen direction. They end up in the jobs they do because they counterbalance an organization's natural tendency towards distraction, ADD and momentum dissipation."

This is a sophisticated sentence. But if you strip the essay of its sophisticated language, the core argument is: most CEOs are unintellectual dummies whose sole purpose is to keep the company on track, rowing in a single direction.

Rao's piece is ok if your goal is to read for entertainment. But it's not ok if your goal is to read for career reasons.

---

## The Takeaway

This aspect of critical reading isn't emphasised as much as it should be. I think we are all the poorer for it.

A year ago I wrote *Writing Doesn't Make You a Genius*. I noticed that people tend to assume good writers are smarter than they actually are. I argued that this was mistaken — that writers sound smarter on paper because the act of writing forces them to clarify their ideas.

**But now I have another reason. Writers are often seen as smarter because good writers today are trained to optimise for sounding insightful.** This bleeds over into reader perception.

Whether a writer sounds smart or a piece sounds sophisticated shouldn't affect you if your goal is to put things you read to practice. The questions remain the same:

- "Is this person believable?"
- "How likely is this going to be useful?"
- "What's the cheapest way to find out?"

---

## Key Takeaways for Evaluating Startup Advice

1. **Stories ≠ Truth** — A good story makes something sound insightful, but doesn't make it true. Always ask: is this actionable?

2. **Repackaged Clichés** — If an essay feels like "insight! insight! insight!" keep scrolling, pause. The writer is repackaging obvious truths to keep you engaged.

3. **Sophisticated Language** — Strip away the complicated words. Is the underlying argument useful? Or does it fall apart?

4. **Believability Matters** — Who is this person? Have they done what they're advising? What's their track record?

5. **Cheapest Test** — What's the cheapest way to test if this advice is true for your situation? Don't just believe. Test.

6. **Entertainment vs. Education** — Some essays are entertainment dressed as education. That's fine, but know which you're consuming.

---

One of the things I was trying to do with my series on [Chinese businessmen](https://commoncog.com/the-chinese-businessman-paradox/) was to describe an approach to doing business that isn’t rooted in startup culture.
Doing a startup is now a hot, aspirational, sexy goal for a sizeable percentage of my friends. It was far less sexy back when I was an 18 year old, reading Paul Graham essays on my bed when I should have been studying. I know people today who think that starting a company is their way out of the corporate rat race. And the place they look to for inspiration? Silicon Valley startup culture.
But here’s the catch: having lived, breathed and optimised for startups for the better part of a decade, I no longer think Silicon Valley culture makes sense for a large swath of people. I’m not even sure startup culture makes sense in Asia. Instead, I currently believe that the startup approach isn’t ideal for many industries, and that it doesn’t work for most businesses.
Granted, this isn't a particular novel observation; anyone who's had any experience running a conventional business would know that received SV wisdom is severely limited. But I do think that it's worth it to wade into the weeds to find the limits of startup wisdom, in case anyone thinks its a good idea to gain a career moat by going into business for themselves.

## Optimising VC Outcomes
Pretty much everything I say here can be best understood through the lens of “startup wisdom is about optimising venture capital (VC) outcomes, so take everything you read about startups with the understanding that it's not about optimising outcomes for you.”
Here's a concrete example: in my last post, I talked about something I called the [SME loop](https://commoncog.com/chinese-businessmen-maybe-strategy-matters/#the-sme-loop), which was my name for a ceiling you'll hit if you attempted to grow a small-to-medium enterprise to a large one. This ceiling is so universal, in fact, that I posited that the SME loop was a fundamental law of business.
Conventional startup wisdom has nothing to say about the SME loop, because growing a business to break out of the SME loop takes at least a decade of work. VCs can't benefit from a business that takes that long to grow large; the vast majority of VC funds are required to return their investments within a 10 year period. That means that most VCs will want their companies to grow rapidly enough to get acquired, or go public, before that 10 year deadline.
It also means that no VC will ever be able to invest in or benefit from the value created by billion dollar Asian conglomerates like Ng Teng Fong’s Far East Organisation, or Robert Kuok’s Wilmar International — business empires that took over four decades to build. These incredibly valuable companies take far too long to return capital for a typical VC fund, and so startup culture — which is built around VC outcomes — ignores these companies as viable paths for success.
Or, to put it more crudely: the canon of startup advice literally pretends these companies do not exist.
This matters to you if you are an individual entrepreneur, because if you only draw lessons from startup culture, you are prematurely and artificially limiting your paths to success. To use a crude illustration, startup culture gives you a set of recommendations that looks like this:
But the reality of business is that the set of possible paths to business success looks like this:
What’s the difference between the startup set and the superset? The answer is time.
Every venture-funded business is in a race against time to grow big and valuable. Normal businesses are not. The false dichotomy that startup culture makes is to imply that the only way to bigness is through bone-crushing speed. This ignores the many Asian conglomerates that were built over two to three decades of consistent execution. It also ignores the South East Asian monopolies that were built by multiple generations of Chinese business families.
In truth, this need for speed exists because startup VCs need to make a return within a certain time period. It also exists because the companies that make the most returns for VCs are companies that are first to win in a winner-takes-all market. Think Google, or Facebook — companies that are so large and powerful they crush virtually all their direct competition after gaining the upper hand.
Startup investors know this, and so they encourage a bunch of counter-intuitive behaviours. For instance, VCs encourage startups to spend money excessively — anything, really, to gain an edge against the competition. When the goal is to win a monopoly in a winner-takes-all market, financial irresponsibility makes sense.
But this goes further: in fact, everything in conventional startup wisdom may be understood through the lens of making a return for investors within a 10 year time period:

- Don’t start a company without a cofounder. (It’s very stressful to grow a valuable company under 10 years).
- Pick a large and growing market that is overlooked. (It’s easier to grow a valuable company under 10 years when a market is growing but overlooked).
- When taking on investment from VCs, play to their fear of missing out. (VCs need home runs to remain solvent).
- Iterate on your product until you find product-market fit — and you’ll know you’ve found product-market fit when you experience [hockey-stick growth](https://davidcummings.org/2010/12/19/hockey-stick-growth-for-startups/?ref=commoncog.com). (Hockey-stick growth is how you grow a valuable company under 10 years).
- Move fast and break things. (Growth above all).
- Don’t worry about bad management at the beginning. (Growth above all).
- Do whatever is necessary to reach the next level of growth. (Growth above all).
- Don’t sell out early when you might grow even bigger and win. (VCs win when one company in their portfolio wins big. So it’s probably better to maximise the upside of each bet they make).
The upshot of all of this is that startups are often a crapshoot. The exhortation to grow at all costs lead to scorched earth practices: you either grow big to become a winner in a large and valuable market … or you die. The ones that don’t win and don’t die live an uncomfortable existence: their investors pressure them to get acqui-hired as a means of recouping some of their investment, or write them off as a bad deal and ignore their calls for help.
This is, I hope, obvious to anyone who’s spent any amount of time watching and thinking about startups.

## Removing the Deadline
Something interesting happens when you remove the artificial constraints of venture capital. Suddenly, the practices that Silicon Valley preaches as necessary to business fades into irrelevance. When you’re not under pressure to build a large and valuable company in a short amount of time, you have way more options available to you.
Links to companies mentioned in this illustration: [Basecamp](https://basecamp.com/?ref=commoncog.com), [Panic](https://panic.com/?ref=commoncog.com), [Indie Hackers](https://www.indiehackers.com/?ref=commoncog.com).These options are wonderfully varied. You may choose to build a profitable business that supports your lifestyle. You may choose to build a profitable business that forms the basis of your business empire — and then strategically take on investment or joint partnerships for your subsidiaries over the course of a few decades ([Robert Kuok](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Kuok?ref=commoncog.com)). You may choose to build and then sell a series of small, profitable internet businesses, eventually parlaying your skills and capital into a go-big-or-go-home company ([Rob Walling](https://wpcurve.com/bootstrapped-drip-into-a-7-figure-saas-business/?ref=commoncog.com)). You may choose to build a secondary source of income while you pursue conventional employment ([Endcrawl](https://www.indiehackers.com/interview/growing-a-saas-app-for-the-film-industry-with-rigorous-experimentation-8aa8348dae?ref=commoncog.com)). You may choose to take on investment only after you’ve gained enough experience building your position in a market ([DS3](https://commoncog.com/breaking-out-of-the-sme-loop-a-story/)). Or you may choose to become a digital nomad ([Jon Yongfook](https://www.yongfook.com/12-startups-in-12-months.html?ref=commoncog.com)).
The truth is that there are an infinite number of ways to build a profitable, rewarding business, and to adapt that business to your personal goals. But because the set of paths are so varied, and because nobody stands to make a fortune from your taking them, nobody writes about these paths.
The path gets the most attention is the path of the startup. And the constraints on doing one are so tight that Silicon Valley often says that there’s “[only one way to do a startup](https://playbook.samaltman.com/?ref=commoncog.com)”.
I’m not arguing that you shouldn’t do a startup. Startups are expected value calculations where the probability of success is low but the payout is high. If you win, you really do win big.
What I’m arguing instead is that startup advice really only applies to a subset of the space of possible businesses you might do. Don’t think that startup advice is the be-all and end-all of business advice. It isn’t; it’s merely a tiny segment you should feel free to ignore.

 Originally published 16 October 2018, last updated 21 July 2022.
